next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

The trophy case outside the school office held dead football seasons under glass, and the front doors nearby kept breathing out weak air-conditioning that smelled like copier heat and old floor wax. Micah stood beside it with the back of his T-shirt stuck to him from shoulder blade to waistband, pretending to read the names etched into little brass plates from years before he was old enough to care. August had that particular Miami meanness where the air did not move unless it was being paid to. It was easier to look at dead football seasons than at the office window where Ms. Francis could see him waiting.

Marcia had sent him with bus fare, one extra dollar for a cold drink if the heat got at him, and exact instructions. Go to the office, pick up the packet, come straight back. Don't stop by the docks. Don't stop by anybody's house. Don't let Dre carry you nowhere stupid. At the sink she had counted the bills twice before folding them into his palm, her fingers cold from rinsing out somebody else's old yogurt container to save for leftovers. Behind her, Kerene had leaned over the hot plate and curled one eyelash with the back of a teaspoon because she still had not replaced the metal curler she swore TSA had stolen. Tiana had been digging through the dresser drawer that was not really hers anymore, moving her document sleeve from one stack of shirts to another like the papers might get legs and leave without her. The room had been full before breakfast. Micah had understood what Marcia meant without her saying it plain. The house did not have room for one mo...
open_hooks_to_carry: The landlord weaponizes procedure rather than shouting.
Elena's help is real but insufficient.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ia a photocopied sheet with rotator-cuff injuries circled in blue pen. It was the only kindness the room could afford.

The drugstore three blocks down had a canvas sling hanging on a peg near the ace bandages. Tiana bought the cheapest one, a bottle of liniment, and generic ibuprofen. By the time they got home, the webbing had already sawn a red line into Delroy's neck. She unwrapped the scarf from her hair, threaded it through the sling strap, and knotted it so the arm rode a little higher and the strap stopped biting skin.

Marcia tucked what remained of the money into an envelope that had once held the light bill and left the front blank because none of her labels fit anymore. Inside, the bills looked more like residue than earnings, the morning reduced to paper and still not enough.

"Tomorrow," she said, rubbing liniment careful and mean across the muscle above his collarbone, "you don't go nowhere lifting anything. You hear me?"

He was already stretched flat on his back because any turn of the torso made the shoulder spark. Tiana shoved a folded towel under the bad arm until the pull eased a little. The apartment smelled of rice, bleach, onions, menthol, and concrete dust now, home and job ground together so fine there was no separating them.

"I hear."

On the milk crate beside the mattress, Tiana squared the blue folder before she killed the light. On the table, under the white sheet of pad paper, the rest of the money made a low untidy shape. It looked smaller every time Delroy glanced at it, and even lying still he could feel how much heavier the arm had become.
state_deltas_required: Keep the family housed after losing Celia's income and Delroy's work capacity.
Kerene arrives with a folding cot, Tiana's room ceases to be hers, and privacy becomes another bill the family cannot pay.
